content.text: Ray is a Python framework that makes it possible to scale code from a single laptop to a cluster of hundreds of machines without rewriting it. It was originally developed at the UC Berkeley RISELab and has since become one of the most widely adopted distributed computing frameworks in the machine learning and data engineering world — used heavily in production by companies like OpenAI, Uber, Spotify, and Shopify.
At its core, Ray is a

built in Python. It lets you take ordinary Python functions and classes, annotate them with simple decorators, and have them run in parallel — either across multiple CPU cores on a single machine, or across dozens of worker nodes in a cloud cluster.

Ray does not force you into a special programming model like MapReduce or Spark's RDD abstraction. Instead, it extends plain Python with a small API surface: remote functions (called "tasks") and remote stateful objects (called "actors"). Everything else — work scheduling, data movement between workers, fault tolerance, cluster lifecycle management — is handled by Ray's internal runtime.
Beyond the core engine, Ray provides a suite of higher-level libraries collectively called the
— scalable data loading and preprocessing pipelines
— distributed model training (PyTorch, TensorFlow, XGBoost, etc.)
These libraries are independently usable and can be composed together, meaning you can build an end-to-end ML pipeline entirely within Ray — from raw data ingestion through training and serving.
Data pipelines in ML systems face a specific combination of challenges that general-purpose distributed systems do not solve well:

A pipeline might include CPU-bound preprocessing, GPU-bound training, and I/O-bound data loading — all happening simultaneously. Traditional batch systems like Spark treat all resources as interchangeable.

Many ML workloads (hyperparameter search, active learning, reinforcement learning) produce task graphs whose shape is not known in advance — the output of one task determines how many follow-on tasks are created. Static DAG executors like Airflow cannot express this.

Iterative algorithms (training loops, simulation environments) need workers to maintain state across many calls. Stateless function pipelines require expensive serialization and data shipping on every call.

Data scientists prototype code locally. Productionizing it traditionally required a full rewrite into a different framework (Spark, Dask, or a custom distributed system). Ray's value proposition is that the same code runs locally for debugging and in a 100-node cluster in production.
The entire distributed programming model reduces to two decorators:
for functions and classes. There is no DAG specification language, no XML config, no special data structure to learn first.
Because Ray uses separate processes (not threads), it bypasses Python's Global Interpreter Lock entirely. CPU-bound tasks run in genuine parallel on all available cores.

Ray actors are remote Python class instances that persist in memory across calls. This is essential for things like parameter servers, simulation environments, database connection pools, and streaming aggregators.
Shared memory via the object store.

Large Python objects (NumPy arrays, Pandas DataFrames) are stored in Ray's distributed in-memory object store using Apache Arrow's shared memory format. Workers on the same machine access this data with zero copy — no serialization overhead at all.
First-class GPU and heterogeneous resource scheduling.
Ray natively understands CPUs, GPUs, and custom resource types. You declare that a task needs 1 GPU and Ray ensures it only runs on a node that has one available.

Ray integrates with virtually every major ML library: PyTorch, TensorFlow, Hugging Face Transformers, XGBoost, LightGBM, scikit-learn, Dask, Spark, Pandas, and cloud object stores (S3, GCS, Azure Blob).
Ray workers and the object store must be initialized before any work runs. For tiny jobs (milliseconds of compute), the overhead of launching Ray completely swamps the computation.
The object store pins large objects in shared memory (backed by
on Linux). Systems with limited RAM or many concurrent pipelines can run into memory pressure if object lifetimes are not managed carefully.

When tasks fail in a remote process, the traceback arrives through Ray's task scheduler rather than propagating directly. Stack traces can be truncated, and debugging distributed deadlocks is significantly harder than debugging local code.
Every argument and return value of a Ray task must be serializable (by pickle or Arrow). Code that relies on unpicklable objects (open file handles, locks, some C extensions) requires workarounds.

Running Ray at scale requires understanding its cluster launcher, autoscaler, head/worker node topology, and cloud provider integrations. This operational overhead is real, even though Ray abstracts most of it.
is called, Ray starts several key processes:
— a per-node scheduling daemon. Accepts task submissions, manages local resource availability, and communicates with other raylets to find workers for remote tasks.
— a shared memory server (using Apache Plasma) that stores task outputs. Workers on the same node can read these outputs with zero copy using mmap.
— a centralized metadata store (backed by Redis in multi-node deployments, or in-memory for single-node). Tracks actor locations, object lineage, and cluster membership.
— a web UI showing cluster resource utilization, task timelines, and object store usage.
A Ray task is an annotated Python function that returns an
(a future) immediately when called. The actual computation happens asynchronously in a remote worker process.

import ray

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def square(x: int) -> int:
    return x * x

# This returns immediately — ref is a future, not the result
ref = square.remote(5)

# ray.get() blocks until the task completes and fetches the result
result = ray.get(ref)  # 25
, Ray serializes the argument, submits the task to the scheduler, and returns an
. The scheduler finds an idle worker process (or spawns one), deserializes the argument, runs the function, serializes the result into the object store, and marks the
retrieves it from the object store.
When a task's argument is itself an
, Ray builds a dependency graph automatically — the downstream task will not run until the upstream task completes.

@ray.remote
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b

x_ref = square.remote(3)   # runs: 3 → 9
y_ref = square.remote(4)   # runs: 4 → 16
z_ref = add.remote(x_ref, y_ref)  # waits for both, then runs: 9+16 → 25

result = ray.get(z_ref)  # 25
— Ray builds the DAG at runtime from how
values are passed between remote calls.
Actors are remote stateful objects. When you decorate a class with
, Ray provisions a dedicated worker process for each actor instance. Method calls on the actor run sequentially in that process, preserving state.

The actor process lives until it is explicitly deleted or the driver exits. Multiple driver processes can hold references to the same actor and call its methods concurrently (Ray queues the calls and processes them one at a time within the actor, maintaining consistency).
When a task returns a large NumPy array, Ray stores it in the Plasma object store — a region of

shared between all Ray workers on the same machine. Any worker that needs this object gets a direct memory-mapped view with no copying. This is critical for data pipelines: a 1 GB array can be "passed" between ten tasks on the same node without ever being copied.
Across nodes, objects are transferred automatically via gRPC when a remote worker needs data that lives on a different node.
Here is the flow of a Ray task from submission to completion:---
Ray Data is the library within Ray specifically designed for data pipeline workloads: loading, transforming, and feeding data into ML training or serving. It is the Ray equivalent of
, Spark DataFrames, or Dask DataFrames, but designed to integrate natively with the Ray ecosystem.

is a distributed, lazy collection of records. Conceptually it is similar to a Spark RDD or a Dask DataFrame, but records can be arbitrary Python objects, Arrow record batches, NumPy arrays, or Pandas DataFrames. Internally, Ray Data organizes data into
— fixed-size partitions that are stored as objects in the Ray object store.

import ray

# Read from various sources — all lazy (no data loaded yet)
ds = ray.data.read_parquet("s3://my-bucket/data/*.parquet")
ds = ray.data.read_csv("data/train.csv")
ds = ray.data.read_images("data/images/")
ds = ray.data.from_items([{"x": i, "label": i % 2} for i in range(10_000)])
are lazy — they build a logical plan but do not execute until data is consumed (by iteration, writing, or explicit materialization).

import ray
import numpy as np

ds = ray.data.read_parquet("s3://my-bucket/train.parquet")

# Map: apply a function to each row (Arrow batch by default)
def normalize(batch: dict) -> dict:
    batch["feature"] = (batch["feature"] - batch["feature"].mean()) / batch["feature"].std()
    return batch

# map_batches applies fn to Arrow batches — efficient for vectorized ops
ds = ds.map_batches(normalize, batch_format="numpy")

# filter: keep only rows matching a predicate
ds = ds.filter(lambda row: row["label"] != -1)

# flat_map: one row → many rows
ds = ds.flat_map(lambda row: [{"token": t} for t in row["text"].split()])
A key design principle of Ray Data is
— data flows through the pipeline in blocks rather than being fully materialized at each stage. This allows pipelines that process datasets far larger than available RAM.

# Iterate without materializing the full dataset in memory
for batch in ds.iter_batches(batch_size=256, batch_format="numpy"):
    # Only `batch_size` rows are in memory at a time
    model.train_step(batch)
Ray Data supports running preprocessing steps on GPUs by specifying resource requirements on
How to implement: building a Ray data pipeline

pip install "ray[data]"           # Ray + Ray Data
pip install "ray[data,train]"     # + distributed training
pip install "ray[default]"        # + dashboard, cluster launcher

import ray

# Single-machine (uses all local cores and RAM)
ray.init()

# Connect to existing cluster
ray.init(address="ray://my-cluster-head:10001")

# With resource constraints
ray.init(num_cpus=8, num_gpus=2)
Step 2 — build a simple parallel pipeline
This example applies a CPU-bound transformation to a large dataset in parallel across all available cores:

import ray
import pandas as pd
import time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def process_chunk(df_ref) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = ray.get(df_ref)
    # Simulate expensive preprocessing
    df["feature_sq"] = df["feature"] ** 2
    df["normalized"] = (df["feature"] - df["feature"].mean()) / df["feature"].std()
    return df

# Load 1M rows and split into 10 chunks
df = pd.DataFrame({"feature": range(1_000_000), "label": [i % 2 for i in range(1_000_000)]})
chunks = [df.iloc[i*100_000:(i+1)*100_000].copy() for i in range(10)]

# Put chunks in object store once — avoid re-serializing
chunk_refs = [ray.put(chunk) for chunk in chunks]

# Launch all tasks in parallel (non-blocking)
result_refs = [process_chunk.remote(ref) for ref in chunk_refs]

# Collect when needed
results = ray.get(result_refs)
final_df = pd.concat(results, ignore_index=True)
print(f"Processed {len(final_df):,} rows")

import ray

ray.init()

# 1. Ingest
ds = ray.data.read_parquet("s3://ml-data/features/")

# 2. Transform
def extract_features(batch: dict) -> dict:
    import numpy as np
    arr = np.array(batch["raw_feature"])
    batch["log_feature"] = np.log1p(arr)
    batch["sqrt_feature"] = np.sqrt(arr)
    return batch

ds = ds.map_batches(extract_features, batch_format="numpy")

# 3. Filter out bad rows
ds = ds.filter(lambda row: row["log_feature"] is not None)

# 4. Repartition for balanced parallelism downstream
ds = ds.repartition(num_blocks=200)

# 5. Write output
ds.write_parquet("s3://ml-data/processed/")

print(ds.schema())
print(f"Total rows: {ds.count()}")
Step 4 — using actors for stateful pipeline stages
When a pipeline stage needs shared state (e.g., a model loaded into memory, a database connection pool, or a running tally), use actors:

import ray
from collections import defaultdict

ray.init()

@ray.remote
class FeatureStore:
    """Stateful actor that accumulates statistics for online normalization."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.sums = defaultdict(float)
        self.counts = defaultdict(int)
    
    def update(self, batch: dict) -> None:
        for col, values in batch.items():
            self.sums[col] += sum(values)
            self.counts[col] += len(values)
    
    def get_mean(self, col: str) -> float:
        return self.sums[col] / self.counts[col] if self.counts[col] > 0 else 0.0

# Create a persistent actor
store = FeatureStore.remote()

@ray.remote
def process_with_stats(batch: dict, store_handle) -> dict:
    # Update shared statistics
    store_handle.update.remote({"value": batch["value"]})
    return batch

# Process batches, all reporting to the same actor
batches = [{"value": [i * 0.1 for i in range(100)]} for _ in range(20)]
refs = [process_with_stats.remote(b, store) for b in batches]
ray.get(refs)

mean = ray.get(store.get_mean.remote("value"))
print(f"Running mean: {mean:.4f}")
lets you process results as they complete rather than blocking on the slowest one:

import ray
import random, time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def slow_task(i: int) -> int:
    time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 2.0))
    return i * i

refs = [slow_task.remote(i) for i in range(20)]
remaining = refs[:]
results = []

while remaining:
    # Get up to 5 results as they complete
    done, remaining = ray.wait(remaining, num_returns=5, timeout=1.0)
    for ref in done:
        results.append(ray.get(ref))
    print(f"Got {len(results)} so far, {len(remaining)} remaining...")

print(results)

import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def train_model(learning_rate: float, batch_size: int) -> dict:
    # Simulate model training
    score = 1.0 / (learning_rate * 100) - (batch_size / 512.0) * 0.1
    score += np.random.normal(0, 0.01)
    return {"lr": learning_rate, "batch": batch_size, "val_score": round(score, 4)}

lrs = [1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3]
batches = [32, 64, 128, 256]

refs = [train_model.remote(lr, bs) for lr in lrs for bs in batches]
results = ray.get(refs)
best = max(results, key=lambda r: r["val_score"])
print(f"Best config: lr={best['lr']}, batch={best['batch']}, score={best['val_score']}")

Consider a production ML feature pipeline at a mid-size company processing 50 GB of user behavioral data each night. The pipeline must: read raw JSON events from S3, parse and validate records, compute rolling aggregates per user, join with a static user metadata table, normalize features, and write Arrow/Parquet files for the training job.
A Ray cluster with 1 head node and 8 worker nodes (each with 32 CPUs and 128 GB RAM) is provisioned using the Ray autoscaler on AWS.
The driver script starts the pipeline:

This pipeline runs in approximately 18 minutes on the 8-node cluster versus 4+ hours single-threaded. The streaming execution model ensures memory usage stays bounded — only a few blocks are in memory at once — even though the total data is 50 GB.
The most common performance mistake is collecting results immediately after each task submission, eliminating parallelism entirely:

# WRONG — each task runs sequentially
results = []
for i in range(100):
    ref = compute.remote(i)
    results.append(ray.get(ref))  # blocks here — kills parallelism

# CORRECT — submit all, collect all
refs = [compute.remote(i) for i in range(100)]
results = ray.get(refs)
Passing large objects as task arguments.
When a large object (DataFrame, model weights) is passed directly as a function argument, Ray serializes it for every task. Use
to store it once in the object store and pass the reference instead:

# WRONG — df is serialized 100 times
for i in range(100):
    process.remote(large_df, i)

# CORRECT — stored once, read by reference
df_ref = ray.put(large_df)
for i in range(100):
    process.remote(df_ref, i)
Using actors for trivially stateless work.
Actors have higher overhead than plain tasks (a dedicated process must stay alive). Using them for stateless transformations wastes resources.
Tasks without resource declarations are assumed to use 1 CPU. A task that actually uses 8 cores or 1 GPU will cause resource contention and correctness issues if not declared with
should be called explicitly when done, especially when
was called multiple times across cells.
Creating tasks inside tasks (nested remotes) carelessly.

Nested task creation is legal in Ray but each nested task competes for the same global resource pool. Deep nesting without explicit resource accounting can deadlock a cluster where every worker is waiting for a child task that can never start.
Any large object used by many tasks should be placed in the object store once via
. Workers on the same node will access it zero-copy.
Size batches to match cache lines.

For data pipeline tasks, batch sizes between 256 and 4096 rows typically achieve the best balance between task overhead and memory efficiency. Very small batches create scheduler overhead; very large batches create memory pressure and reduce parallelism.
When using Ray Data, always prefer
with NumPy or Pandas operations over
on individual rows. Vectorized operations over batches can be 100x faster than per-row Python loops.
. The task timeline (waterfall view) immediately reveals scheduling gaps, load imbalance, and unexpectedly slow tasks.
on tasks that return multiple values.
When a task returns a tuple, declaring
, allowing downstream tasks to consume them independently without materializing the full tuple.
Whenever tasks have variable runtimes (network I/O, model inference, database queries),
prevents slow stragglers from blocking fast results.
that use stateful objects (e.g., a loaded model), pass
rather than creating N actors manually. Ray Data manages lifecycle and load balancing automatically.

Ray's task scheduling overhead is approximately 1–5 ms per task depending on object sizes and cluster topology. This means tasks with less than ~10 ms of compute are too fine-grained for Ray — the overhead exceeds the benefit. The correct granularity is tasks representing at least hundreds of milliseconds to seconds of work.

Ray's autoscaler monitors cluster resource utilization and automatically adds or removes worker nodes. When tasks are pending due to resource constraints, the autoscaler provisions new VMs (on AWS, GCP, or Azure) and joins them to the cluster within 1–3 minutes. When utilization drops, idle nodes are terminated. This makes Ray well suited to batch workloads with highly variable resource demands.

The Ray object store uses LRU eviction when memory pressure builds. Objects that are no longer referenced by any live task or actor handle are eligible for eviction. Objects can also be spilled to disk (NVMe SSD recommended) when RAM is exhausted, with automatic restore on next access. In production,
should be set to 60–70% of available RAM to leave headroom for worker process heap allocations.

Ray has been validated at scales of 1,000+ nodes and 100,000+ concurrent tasks in production. The GCS becomes a bottleneck at very high task submission rates (>100,000 tasks/second) — at this scale, task submission should be batched, or the
calls should be made from worker processes rather than a single driver.
For datasets larger than cluster RAM,
must be consumed in streaming mode. Using
on a large dataset forces full materialization — this will OOM. Use
. Shows cluster resource utilization, per-task timelines, actor states, object store usage, and logs.
— submits Ray programs to a cluster as isolated jobs with their own environments and lifecycle tracking. Replaces manual SSH-and-run approaches for production deployment.

— cluster lifecycle management for AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes (via KubeRay), and on-premises clusters. Configured via a YAML file specifying node types, head node, worker types, and scaling policies.
— the official Kubernetes operator for Ray. Manages
custom resources. The standard deployment method for Ray in production Kubernetes environments.
— Ray Train wraps PyTorch DDP for distributed training
has native Hugging Face Dataset integration; Ray Train has
— replaces Dask's scheduler with Ray, enabling Dask DataFrames to run on Ray clusters without rewriting
— RayDP allows Spark DataFrames to run as Ray tasks
— Ray Tune integrates with both for experiment tracking
— drop-in Pandas replacement that uses Ray as its execution engine

Dask is better for analytics workloads dominated by DataFrame operations — its pandas API compatibility is deeper. Ray is better when pipelines include non-DataFrame work (model inference, custom C++ ops, simulation), require actors, or need GPU heterogeneity.

Spark excels at SQL-style batch ETL over structured data and has a mature ecosystem for data warehousing. Ray is better for ML-heavy pipelines, Python-native code that cannot be expressed as Spark operations, and workloads requiring stateful streaming.

Celery is a task queue optimized for web application background jobs (email sending, thumbnail generation). It has no support for data-parallel computation, shared memory, or GPU resources. Ray is the correct choice for scientific or ML compute.
Ray automatically retries failed tasks. By default, a task that raises an exception is marked as failed and the exception is re-raised when
. Tasks can be configured to retry automatically on system failures (node crashes, network partitions) with
Actor failures are detected by the GCS through heartbeat monitoring. When an actor's node crashes, the GCS marks the actor as dead. Actors can be configured to restart automatically with
Object lineage is tracked so that objects lost due to node failure can be recomputed from their inputs (similar to Spark's lineage-based fault recovery).
By default, Ray's inter-node communication (gRPC between raylets, object transfer, GCS replication) is
. In production, Ray should be deployed inside a private VPC with security group rules that prevent external access to Ray ports (6379, 8265, 10001, etc.). TLS can be enabled for the Ray client (
) but full mTLS across the cluster requires additional configuration.
When using the Ray Jobs API or Ray Serve, requests should be authenticated at the reverse proxy (Nginx, AWS ALB) layer. Ray itself does not perform authentication on incoming job submissions.

Ray tasks and actors run in worker processes that inherit the environment of the head/worker node. Secrets (API keys, database passwords) should be passed via environment variables injected at cluster launch time through the Ray runtime environment, rather than embedded in task arguments or code:

Ray does not provide container-level isolation between tasks by default — all worker processes run under the same OS user. For multi-tenant deployments where different teams share a cluster, KubeRay with Kubernetes namespaces and resource quotas provides proper isolation.
Ray turns ordinary Python functions and classes into distributed tasks and actors with two decorators, requiring minimal code changes to scale from a laptop to a cluster.
The object store with shared memory (Apache Plasma/Arrow) eliminates data copying between co-located workers, making large-array workloads extremely efficient.
too early is the single most common mistake — always submit all tasks before collecting results.
Ray Data provides a lazy, streaming, block-based pipeline abstraction that processes datasets larger than available RAM without out-of-memory errors.
Ray natively understands heterogeneous resources (CPUs, GPUs, custom types), making it the natural choice for ML pipelines that mix preprocessing, training, and serving.
The full Ray AIR suite (Data, Train, Tune, Serve) enables end-to-end ML pipelines within a single framework, reducing the operational complexity of gluing together disparate systems.

In production, Ray should be deployed in a private network (VPC), secrets injected via runtime environments, and autoscaling managed via KubeRay for Kubernetes or the Ray cluster launcher for cloud VMs.
